activityOfUser.occupation: Tvořím junior.guru, které pomáhá juniorním programátorům

text:
This is either brilliant or scary: Anthropic accidentally leaked the TypeScript source code of Claude Code (which is closed source). Repos sharing the source are taken down with DMCA - as they share copyrighted code that they are not allowed to do so. This is all very standard until this point.

BUT where things take a twist: this one repo rewrote the code using Python, and so it violates no copyright & cannot be taken down with DMCA requests!

The brilliance: copyright does not protect certain kinds of derived works. Rewriting TypeScript code in Python means copyright probably doesn't apply...?

The scary thing: it can be done in trivial amount of time, with AI agents. This one was done with Codex.

This can be done not just for this specific codebase, but any codebase. So what happens with copyright? Will it evolve with AI, or be stuck pre-AI?

If any company is a good one to test on what happens: it is Anthropic. AI labs greatly benefit from derived works not being considered as copyrightable: this is one of the reasons they can freely train on copyrighted work, after all. This repo is doing the same: taking copyrighted code by Anthropic (that Anthropic publicly shared!) and deriving it, transforming into another, common programming language.

You can imagine Anthropic being in a pickle right now:

1. Do they just leave this, and look the other way? Ignoring that it's not exactly fair to transform their code and leave it up there, not wanting to poke the bear.

2. Or do they risk it and poke the bear: claim that claim copyright applies because the work is very clearly derived and mimics the original... but this could be bad for their own business in much bigger ways! E.g. imagine regulation coming into play that bans this (transforming code from one language to another.) Claude Code and other tools would have to refuse this kind of generation - and become a lot less useful. Lawsuits against AI labs could spike against labs like Anthropic and others. The losses from #2 could eclipse having this repo stay up. And you can bet it would be a high-profile case: an AI lab arguing copyright needs to be updated and explanded thanks to AI agents!

So my bet is #1 happens. Not the interest of an AI lab to expand copyright protections to derived work cretated by an LLM...

The repo: https://lnkd.in/eGzp_DSZ

And here are details about how Anthropic builds Claude Code, from a deepdive in The Pragmatic Engineer: https://lnkd.in/eBvzDmRK (they move very fast, with 60-100 internal releases per day, and so the accidentally leaked code is unlikely to be relevant for too long, as in the comments Rodrigo Pimentel also pointed out [thank you!] )

comments.text:
If it's not done in a "clean room" setup, it will most likely not hold in court. Some emulators were able to get over copyright claims because they could demonstrate they implemented everything from scratch without using anything more than the observed behavior of the system as a black box. Once you have the original code, those claims are hard to prove.
It could be enough to deter the usual copyright claims on GitHub, but this most likely won't hold in any court.

author.occupation: Software Engineer / Product Developer at Smarter Ecommerce (smec)

comments.text: A missing .npmignore entry shipping 512,000 lines of unobfuscated TypeScript to the public npm registry is the kind of infosec story that will be featured in engineering onboarding decks for years. What makes it fascinating isnt the leak itself but what the code revealed: deliberate anti-detection systems built specifically to hide internal codenames. The gap between whats on the marketing page and what actually runs in prod is always wider than anyone expects..
What comes to mind is "play stupid games, win stupid prizes."

The intellectual property angle here is going to define the next decade of software law. When AI can translate proprietary code from one language to another in hours, traditional trade secret protection gets murky fast. It is not just Anthropic’s problem — any company building on top of AI-generated work is sitting on uncertain legal ground. This case is going to set a precedent that every enterprise AI team needs to watch closely. The organizations that start looping in legal now, before deploying significant AI-generated code to production, will be in a much stronger position when the dust settles.

author.occupation: Technology Executive | Agentic AI, Digital Platforms, Product Engineering & Customer Experience Transformation

comments.text: seems they deleted the repo and now its targeting claw-bot :D

Thank you Gergely Orosz. The copyright angle is very interesting but the one more question is what this means for agent security architecture. System prompts, permission layers, feature flags — all now public. Any agent that relies on obscurity for its security boundaries just lost that layer permanently. The takeaway for anyone building agents: assume your internals will be exposed. Design permission scoping and blast radius containment as if every line of your agent code is already on GitHub.

profile.occupation: Deepdives on software engineering, tech careers and industry trends. Writing The Pragmatic Engineer, the #1 software engineering newsletter on Substack. Author of The Software Engineer’s Guidebook.

comments.text: if AI can rewrite any codebase across languages trivially, the whole concept of code as IP needs a serious rethink.

comments.text:
Nobody created a legit reverse engineered work.  To do that you must only use complied third party set of documents defining the standards, interfaces that are then used by someone that has not seen the original source code. 

That Python and Rust are not clean implementations and would never survive a legal challenge.  They had the source code and told Claude to reimplement in said new language.  That fails on every definition of reverse engineering.

author.occupation: Director of Reliability, Cloud Architect at Filevine

comments.text: The deeper implication here is for every company shipping proprietary algorithms. If an AI agent can rewrite a codebase across languages in hours, the moat was never the code — it was the data, the domain expertise encoded in architectural decisions, and the institutional knowledge of why certain tradeoffs were made. The Python port preserves syntax but loses context. That gap is where defensibility actually lives.

author.occupation: CTO & Co-Founder, Boosted.ai | Transitioning engineering teams to AI-accelerated development | PhD Theoretical Chemistry, Cornell

comments.text:
> So what happens with copyright?
Nothing. It is too late. Legislators were warned to create solutions ahead of what was coming. Elon Musk said in 2018 that it was already too late - and it was.
As always, the legal system failed to provide a solution in time. Don't worry, engineers will create a solution for that as well.
See Joe Rogan & Elon Musk talk from 2018:
https://youtu.be/Ra3fv8gl6NE
